EeroQ is building next-generation quantum devices by combining the tiny size of electrons and superfluid helium which is the cleanest environment in nature with CMOS infrastructure and the lack of any need for modular interconnects.
Since receiving fifty identical copies of our Wonder Lake chips in December 2023, we've been working at our Chicago headquarters, trapping and measuring electrons on them.
So far, we've been able to isolate and measure small packets of fewer than 10 electrons on our chips, and can controllably shuttle around those packets to predetermined locations on the device.
The work we have accomplished at EeroQ is a significant step on the road to building a commercially viable quantum computer and has allowed us to pursue our next near-term goals:

occurrence_sentence: EeroQ makes world-first breakthrough in electron qubits floating on helium

EeroQ, the quantum computing company, today announced the first-ever demonstration of the strong coupling of a microwave photon to the charge qubit state of an electron on helium, a landmark result that establishes the electron-on-helium (eHe) approach to quantum computing as a reality and introduces a powerful new contender in the global race to build a practical quantum computer.

EeroQ, the quantum computing company, today announced the first-ever demonstration of the strong coupling of a microwave photon to the charge qubit state of an electron on helium, a landmark result that establishes the electron-on-helium (eHe) approach to quantum computing as a reality and introduces a powerful new contender in the global race to build a practical quantum computer.

role_in_text: company that made a breakthrough in electron qubits
occurrence_sentence: EeroQ's quantum measurement demonstration now establishes qubits based on electrons on helium as a viable seventh candidate for quantum computing;
"This is a seminal moment for quantum computing," said Nick Farina, co-founder and CEO of EeroQ.

role_in_text: researchers at Bell Labs and Michigan State University first conceived of the concept of using electrons floating on the surface of liquid helium as qubits
occurrence_sentence: The concept of using electrons floating on the surface of liquid helium as qubits was first conceived of in 1999 by researchers at Bell Labs and Michigan State University and published in Science, and later expanded upon by EeroQ's CTO, Princeton professor Stephen Lyon.

role_in_text: researchers at Bell Labs and Michigan State
